---
title: "Build and deploy a stroke prediction model using R"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: html_document
author: "Johnny Nguyen"
---

# About Data Analysis Report

This RMarkdown file contains the report of the data analysis done for the project on building and deploying a stroke prediction model in R. It contains analysis such as data exploration, summary statistics and building the prediction models. The final report was completed on `r date()`. 

**Data Description:**

According to the World Health Organization (WHO) stroke is the 2nd leading cause of death globally, responsible for approximately 11% of total deaths.

This data set is used to predict whether a patient is likely to get stroke based on the input parameters like gender, age, various diseases, and smoking status. Each row in the data provides relevant information about the patient.


# Task One: Import data and data preprocessing

install.packages("tidyverse")
install.packages(c("skimr", "naniar", "mice", "corrplot"))

## Load data and install packages
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
library(naniar)
library(skimr)

stroke_data <- read_csv("Build-deploy-stroke-prediction-model-R/healthcare-dataset-stroke-data.csv", 
                        na = c("N/A", "Unknown", ""))

## Describe and explore the data
dim(stroke_data)
head(stroke_data)

#clean and fix the data
# Convert categorical and binary columns to factors
stroke_clean <- stroke_data %>%
  select(-id) %>% 
  mutate(across(c(gender, hypertension, heart_disease, ever_married, work_type, Residence_type, smoking_status, stroke), as.factor)) %>%
  mutate(bmi = as.numeric(bmi))


# m=5 creates 5 different versions to test stability (Multiple Imputation)
# method='pmm' (Predictive Mean Matching) - looked at a patient's age and glucose and found "neighbors" with similar stats to guess their BMI.
library(mice)
imputed_model <- mice(stroke_clean, m=5, method='pmm', seed=123)
# will take the best calculated values and fills the hole - (Polytomous Regression) looked at age and work type to calculate the most likely category for those "Unknown" entries.
stroke_final <- complete(imputed_model, 1)
skim(stroke_final)


str(stroke_final)

stroke_final <- stroke_final %>% filter(gender != "Other")
stroke_final$gender <- droplevels(stroke_final$gender)
table(stroke_final$gender)





# Task Two: Build prediction models
install.packages("tidymodels")
library(tidymodels)

set.seed(123)
# 2. Create a "Split" object (80% for training, 20% for testing)
stroke_split <- initial_split(stroke_final, prop = 0.80, strata = stroke)
train_data <- training(stroke_split) 
test_data  <- testing(stroke_split)

nrow(train_data) #checking if split works
nrow(test_data)
prop.table(table(train_data$stroke)) #check if stroke percent is correct
prop.table(table(test_data$stroke))


install.packages("themis")
library(themis)
# creates a plan to predict stroke using all other variables found in the 
# training set, following the next step
stroke_blueprint <- recipe(stroke ~ . , data = train_data) %>%

# Since cannot process on words like "private", turn categories into 0s and 1s. 
# Find every column that is a category (factor) and turn it into 0s and 1s
# Handles one-hot encode cate var
step_dummy(all_nominal_predictors()) %>%

# Needs variation to measure to process the model by auditing the columns and
# removes any variables that are constant
step_zv(all_predictors()) %>%

# Scales every number so the mean is 0 and Std is 1. In order to avoid the model
# to think that a bigger number is more significant. (Scaling)
step_normalize(all_numeric_predictors()) %>%

# Balancing the training data for a 50/50 split in order to amplify the signal
# of the stroke cases. Creates fake stroke cases to allow the model to learn the signs
# Class Imbalance is fixed and needs to occur at the end to avoid data leakage
step_smote(stroke)

prepped_data <- prep(stroke_blueprint) %>% bake(new_data = NULL)
table(prepped_data$stroke)
# model can no longer guess "No Stroke" to get a high score, will learn the 
# clinical differences between the two groups.




# Creating the algorithm
install.packages("ranger")
# Defines the engine
rf <- rand_forest(trees = 500) %>%
  set_engine("ranger") %>%
  set_mode("classification")
  
# Creates the workflow, combining blueprint and engine for the model
stroke_workflow <- workflow() %>%
  add_recipe(stroke_blueprint) %>%
  add_model(rf)



# Cross-Validation, sees how the model performs on subsets

set.seed(456)
# creates mini-tests from the training data
stroke_minitest <- vfold_cv(train_data, v = 10, strata = stroke)
# Runs the train data across the 10 mini tests
stroke_outcome <- fit_resamples(stroke_workflow,
  resamples = stroke_minitest,
  metrics = metric_set(roc_auc, sensitivity, specificity)
  )
# roc_auc is the grade, distinguishing btewwen stoke and non stroke patients
# sensistivity is the stroke rate that is correctly caught
# specificity chekcs how accuarater the model identifies healthy people as healthy

collect_metrics(stroke_outcome)


# testing for unseen patients
# train data on all 4,087 rows after Cross-validation
final_results <- last_fit(stroke_workflow, stroke_split)

#  shows the performance of the TEST data for the unseen patients
final_performance <- collect_metrics(final_results)
final_predictions <- collect_predictions(final_results)

print(final_performance)


# Prediction count (predicted outcome vs Actual outcome)
final_predictions %>%
  conf_mat(truth = stroke, estimate = .pred_class) %>%
  autoplot(type = "heatmap") +
  labs(title = "Final Test: Confusion Matrix",
       subtitle = "High Sensitivity, Low Specificity")
       
# identifying healthy people (904 out of 965). not giving many false alarms.
# Out of 57 actual stroke patients, the model only caught 13. It missed 44 people.



# Iterative Process to fix (tuning the Random Forest)
# More sensitive noew by lowering requirements by predicting probability
test_prob <- predict(final_results$.workflow[[1]], test_data, type = "prob") %>%
  bind_cols(test_data %>% select(stroke)) %>%
# Instead of prob = 50%, will be > 10% to predict a stroke
  mutate(pred_custom = ifelse(.pred_1 >= 0.1, "1", "0")) %>%
  mutate(pred_custom = factor(pred_custom, levels = c("0", "1")))

test_prob %>%
  conf_mat(truth = stroke, estimate = pred_custom) %>%
  autoplot(type = "heatmap") +
  labs(title = "(10% Threshold)", subtitle = "Lowering the alarm bar to catch more strokes")
# went from missing 44 people to only missing 6.
# gives a "false alarm" to 415 healthy people, which is better than a missed stroke.

# The initial model had high accuracy but failed to identify the majority of stroke cases By adjusting the model's sensitivity, we achieved an 89.5% detection rate, missing only 6 #out of 57 actual strokes. While this increased the false-positive rate to 43%, the clinical priority is to minimize missed strokes (False Negatives) due to the high cost of life associated with a missed diagnosis."



# Logisitic Regression
log_spec <- logistic_reg() %>%
  set_engine("glm") %>%
  set_mode("classification")

log_workflow <- workflow() %>%
  add_recipe(stroke_blueprint) %>% 
  add_model(log_spec)

log_results <- last_fit(log_workflow, stroke_split)

# Compare the AUC
collect_metrics(log_results)

log_results %>%
  collect_predictions() %>%
  conf_mat(truth = stroke, estimate = .pred_class) %>%
  autoplot(type = "heatmap")

# cut the False Alarms (False Positives) nearly in half—from 415 down to 21
# catching 72% of strokes, identifies 41 out of 57 strokes while only bothering 
# 217 healthy people.





# Task Three: Evaluate and select prediction models

metrics <- metric_set(bal_accuracy, f_meas, roc_auc, sens, spec)

# Logistic Reg results
log_eval <- log_results %>%
  collect_predictions() %>%
  metrics(truth = stroke, .pred_1, estimate = .pred_class, event_level = "second") %>%
  mutate(model = "Logistic Regression")

# Random Forest Results
rf_eval <- final_results %>%
  collect_predictions() %>%
  metrics(truth = stroke, .pred_1, estimate = .pred_class, event_level = "second") %>%
  mutate(model = "Random Forest")
  
# Compares both models
comparison <- bind_rows(log_eval, rf_eval) %>%
  select(model, .metric, .estimate) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = .metric, values_from = .estimate)

print(comparison)





# Task Four: Deploy the prediction model

install.packages("shiny")
install.packages("shinythemes")
install.packages("bslib")
install.packages("ggplot2")
library(shiny)
library(shinythemes)
library(ggplot2)

# 1. Get the model ready (Ensure log_results exists in your environment!)
final_model <- extract_workflow(log_results)

# 2. THE UI
ui <- fluidPage(
  theme = shinytheme("flatly"), # Modern, clean professional theme
  
  titlePanel(tags$div(
    icon("heartbeat", lib = "font-awesome"), 
    " Clinical Stroke Risk Predictor"
  )),
  
  hr(), # Horizontal line for visual separation
  
  sidebarLayout(
    sidebarPanel(
      wellPanel(
        tags$h4("Patient Demographics"),
        numericInput("age", "Age:", value = 50, min = 0, max = 120),
        numericInput("avg_glucose_level", "Avg Glucose Level (mg/dL):", value = 100),
        numericInput("bmi", "BMI:", value = 25)
      ),
      
      wellPanel(
        tags$h4("Medical History"),
        selectInput("hypertension", "History of Hypertension?", choices = c("No" = "0", "Yes" = "1")),
        selectInput("heart_disease", "History of Heart Disease?", choices = c("No" = "0", "Yes" = "1"))
      ),
      
      actionButton("predict", "Calculate Risk Score", 
                   class = "btn-lg btn-primary", 
                   style = "width: 100%; font-weight: bold;")
    ),
    
    mainPanel(
      # Using conditional UI to show results only after clicking 'Predict'
      uiOutput("results_ui")
    )
  )
)

# 3. THE SERVER
server <- function(input, output) {
  
  # Reactive values to store the prediction
  prediction_data <- eventReactive(input$predict, {
    # 1. Create patient data frame
    patient_df <- data.frame(
      age = input$age,
      avg_glucose_level = input$avg_glucose_level,
      bmi = input$bmi,
      hypertension = factor(input$hypertension, levels = c("0", "1")),
      heart_disease = factor(input$heart_disease, levels = c("0", "1")),
      # Add other columns if your model requires them (using defaults from test_data)
      gender = "Female", ever_married = "Yes", work_type = "Private", 
      Residence_type = "Urban", smoking_status = "never smoked"
    )
    
    # 2. Predict
    res <- predict(final_model, patient_df, type = "prob")
    return(res$.pred_1)
  })

  # Dynamic UI to make the right side look "Dashboard-y"
  output$results_ui <- renderUI({
    req(input$predict) # Only show after button is pressed
    
    prob <- prediction_data()
    color <- if(prob < 0.1) "#2ecc71" else if(prob < 0.3) "#f1c40f" else "#e74c3c"
    
    tagList(
      h2("Assessment Summary", style = "margin-top: 0;"),
      div(class = "alert alert-info",
          h4(style = "margin:0;", 
             paste0("Calculated Risk: ", round(prob * 100, 2), "%"))
      ),
      plotOutput("risk_plot", height = "150px"),
      helpText("Note: This model is for educational purposes and should not replace professional medical advice.")
    )
  })

  # The Enhanced Visual Plot
  output$risk_plot <- renderPlot({
    prob <- prediction_data()
    
    ggplot() +
      # Background Bar
      geom_rect(aes(xmin = 0, xmax = 100, ymin = 0, ymax = 1), fill = "#ecf0f1", color = "white") +
      # Risk Bar
      geom_rect(aes(xmin = 0, xmax = prob * 100, ymin = 0, ymax = 1), 
                fill = if(prob < 0.2) "#27ae60" else "#e74c3c") +
      # Styling
      scale_x_continuous(expand = c(0,0), breaks = seq(0, 100, 20), labels = paste0(seq(0, 100, 20), "%")) +
      theme_minimal() +
      theme(panel.grid = element_blank(),
            axis.text.y = element_blank(),
            axis.title = element_blank(),
            plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 16)) +
      labs(title = "Stroke Probability Scale")
  })
}

# 4. RUN
shinyApp(ui = ui, server = server)



# Task Five: Findings and Conclusions

# Throughout this process, I learned that building a helpful model is about much more than just getting a high accuracy score. In medical cases like stroke prediction, a model that simply guesses "no stroke" for everyone might look accurate on paper, but it fails to save lives. I discovered that balancing the data and Balanced Accuracy and AUC—is the only way to ensure the model actually works for the patients who need it most.


# The results were a bit surprising because the simpler Logistic Regression model actually beat the more complex Random Forest. The Logistic Regression was much more reliable at catching actual strokes, reaching a 72% detection rate. This taught me that in healthcare, a clear and steady model is often more useful than a complicated one that makes too many mistakes or misses the most important cases.

# The biggest takeaway from this project is that data science only becomes truly valuable when it is deployed into a tool people can actually use. By building the R Shiny app, I showed how a hospital could take these complex math results and turn them into a simple calculator for doctors. This journey from cleaning messy data to creating a clickable risk-predictor proves that the best models are the ones that are both accurate and easy to use in the real world.
